claim: As of December 2023, all six Algarve dams were below 25% capacity (combined ~34 hm³), estimated to supply only ~8 months of public water needs — making reservoir water levels a seasonal variable affecting water availability for wildlife and lost dogs.
stance_target: seasonal water availability modeling for lost dog search (reservoirs as permanent vs temporary water sources)
scope_conditions: Six Algarve dams; December 2023 data from APA
quoted_support: The Algarve dams — Bravura, Odelouca, Arade and Funcho, in Barlavento; Odeleite and Beliche, in Sotavento — are less than a quarter of capacity, according to reports from the Portuguese Environment Agency (APA). Together they will have no more than 34 cubic hectometers of water... 'It's enough to supply the public network for about eight months', says Professor Nuno Loureiro, researcher at the University of Faro.

claim: Barragem de Bravura (Lagos municipality, Odiáxere) was effectively empty in late 2023 — no irrigation water for 1,800 ha of agricultural land for 3 consecutive years; irrigation canals completely dry.
stance_target: Bravura dam and its irrigation canals as dry hazard features (abandoned infrastructure) in western Algarve territorial layer
scope_conditions: Bravura Dam catchment, Lagos/Odiáxere area; 2021-2023 drought period
quoted_support: In the neighbouring Municipality of Lagos, in the Odiáxere area, water is an old memory. For three years now, the beneficiaries of the Bravura Dam — some 1800 hectares of irrigated land — have not had a drop of water running in the irrigation canals. The dam's reservoir looks like the bottom of a pond and its banks are now authentic arid and stony ravines.
numbers: 1,800 ha irrigated land without water for 3 years
100,000 m³ (2 weeks supply) from October 2023 rainfall

claim: The Algarve has 17 underground aquifers including an estimated 20,000-25,000 boreholes (mostly illegal), providing hidden water sources throughout the region — potential water attractors for lost dogs that are not visible on surface maps.
stance_target: hidden water sources (wells, boreholes) as key attractors in territorial hazard/water mapping for lost dog searches
scope_conditions: Algarve region, 5,400 km²; estimated data from hydrologist José Paulo Monteiro
quoted_support: 6765 water intakes are licensed throughout the Algarve, including boreholes, wells and noras, according to the National Water Resources Information System. But no one knows how many illegal boreholes there are. José Paulo Monteiro, hydrologist, professor at the Centre for Water Sciences and Technologies at the University of Faro, calculates that 'there are between 20 thousand and 25 thousand boreholes in the Algarve, a region with an area of ​​5400Km2'.

claim: The most important Algarve aquifer (Querença-Faro-Silves) is showing saltwater intrusion and has an annual recharge capacity of ~3 million m³/year — already critical, meaning shallow wells and noras in this zone may be partially saline.
stance_target: water quality of inland wells/noras as factors in dog behavioral modeling (salt vs fresh water)
quoted_support: The most important — delimited by Querença, in the Municipality of Loulé, Faro and Silves — has already experienced a 'recharge capacity of the order of three million cubic meters per year'. The water level is dropping so much that salty sea water has already started to infiltrate.

claim: Annual Algarve water consumption is ~237 hm³, of which >half (129 hm³) comes from aquifer extraction — exceeding sustainable yield, meaning dry season availability of surface water near zero and groundwater increasingly stressed.
stance_target: overall water scarcity characterization of Algarve for lost dog search modeling
scope_conditions: Algarve region; data from Nuno Loureiro (Univ. Faro) and APA
quoted_support: The Algarve consumes an average of 237 cubic hectometers of water per year, the equivalent of 237 billion liters. More than half, almost 129 hectometers, comes from aquifers — water that is used for everything: public supply, rule of orchards and golf courses, filling of swimming pools… 'These reserves are going into the red', warns the professor Nuno Loureiro.

claim: Irrigation canal networks in Silves municipality (3,000 ha irrigated) and Odiáxere/Lagos area (1,800 ha, Bravura canal system) form linear infrastructure features that dogs may follow — currently dry in drought years.
stance_target: irrigation canals as terrain corridors (dry or wet) for lost dog movement patterns
scope_conditions: Silves and Lagos municipalities; Barlavento Algarve
quoted_support: The Municipality of Silves, with three thousand hectares of irrigated land, provides land for most of the Algarve's orange groves. Around 150 thousand tons of oranges leave here annually, half of the Algarve's entire production.

claim: The Algarve rain pattern has reversed: historically more rain in Barlavento (Sagres to Albufeira), now Sotavento (Loulé to VRSA) receives more — implying dam capacity distribution and water availability differ from historical maps.
stance_target: updated rainfall distribution knowledge for Algarve territorial layer
scope_conditions: Algarve region; observed trend as of 2023
quoted_support: There was a time when it rained more in the Barlavento Algarve, from Sagres to Albufeira, and less in the Sotavento, from Loulé to Vila Real de Santo António. Now, it's the opposite.
